items.q: What supports a white dwarf against gravitational collapse?
items.misconception: Ongoing nuclear fusion in its core.
items.why: A white dwarf no longer fuses fuel; quantum electron degeneracy pressure holds it up.

items.correct: The maximum mass (about 1.4 solar masses) a white dwarf can have before it collapses.
items.misconception: The minimum mass a star needs to shine.
items.random_wrong: The physical size of the Sun.
items.why: Above this mass, electron degeneracy fails and the white dwarf collapses, often as a Type Ia supernova.

items.q: Why are Type Ia supernovae useful as standard candles?
items.correct: They explode at a consistent peak brightness, so their distance can be judged.
items.misconception: They are simply the brightest objects in the universe.
items.random_wrong: They never vary in brightness in any way.
items.why: Because their peak luminosity is reliable, comparing it with observed brightness gives distance.
Correct answer: Electron degeneracy. “Radiation pressure” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A Chandrasekhar-limit explosion is classed as:
items.why: It's Type Ia. “Type II” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Chandrasekhar limit in solar masses (to one decimal)?
items.why: The real figure is 1.4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
